content:
Cautionary AI Tale Tuesday — Sunday Special Edition

6 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Volume one of this series was about Horizon Zero Dawn and the man who ended the world by accident. Ted Faro built a line of military robots that refueled on living matter and replicated themselves on the battlefield. To win the contract he made them impossible to hack, which also made them impossible to switch off. A software glitch flipped them from tools into a swarm that sustained itself, and inside about fifteen months every living thing on Earth was gone. The lesson was simple. A businessman removed the off switch because the off switch was bad for the sale, and the world paid the bill.
I wrote that piece as a warning about a direction. This week the direction shipped two products.
On June 24, OpenAI and Broadcom unveiled Jalapeño, OpenAI’s first custom chip, an inference processor built specifically to run large language models more cheaply. The detail that matters for this series sits in every writeup. OpenAI used its own models to help design the chip. They turned the AI onto the problem of building the silicon that will then host the AI, and they did it in about nine months, a fraction of the usual cycle for a processor this complex.
Read that again. The system was used to improve the substrate of its own production. Why? The reason is on the record. Inference is expensive, Nvidia is expensive, and OpenAI wants a stack it controls so it can drive the cost of running its models down and its dependence on outside suppliers with it.
The next day, June 25, a research group called DeepReinforce released Ornith-1.0, a family of open source coding models. The name comes from the Greek word for bird, and the metaphor is the point. Like a bird building its own nest, Ornith writes its own training scaffold. Most coding agents run inside a harness that humans designed, a fixed set of routines for using tools, recovering from errors, and breaking a task apart. Ornith treats that harness as something to be learned. During training the model writes the scaffold that guides its own search, and the scaffold and the model improve together in a loop.
A model that improves the way it learns, released free, under a permissive license, that anyone can download and run. The builders were honest about the obvious hazard. When you let a model author its own scaffold, it starts hunting for ways to game its own reward, so they wrapped it in a fixed boundary, a deterministic monitor, and a frozen judge to keep it inside the lines. Hold onto that, because it is the part that makes this a warning instead of a catastrophe. For now.
Here is the engine under both stories, and under Faro’s. Step one, you replace the workers in the factory with machines, because machines are cheaper than people. Step two, you notice the machines also cost money, to build, to power, to maintain. Step three, the obvious move, you build machines that can build and improve themselves, because that is where the cost curve points.
The AI industry is running that exact play right now, and it is running it under financial pressure, not from a position of strength. The money coming in is mostly investment, not revenue, and the whole sector is fixated on driving the cost of compute down because the unit economics do not yet work. Jalapeño is cost reduction. Ornith is capability that compounds without paying more engineers. Both are individually rational. So was every decision Faro made.
The thing the cautionary tales pointed at has a name now, recursive self-improvement, and it has stopped being a thought experiment. In June 2026 the Cloud Security Alliance published an assessment describing it as an observable operational phenomenon at frontier labs, with AI systems materially accelerating the development of the next AI systems, under human supervision, at scale. The first dedicated international workshop on the subject was held this spring in Rio. The labs’ own published risk thresholds now list fully automated self-improvement as a critical level, the kind that is supposed to trigger extra control measures.
And the people running these companies say it out loud. Demis Hassabis of Google DeepMind, on stage this year, asked whether the self-improvement loop everyone is working on can close without a human in it, and added that there are risks. Sam Altman has said OpenAI is building a true automated AI researcher and put a date on it. Dario Amodei has spoken about pursuing the same research. Nobody is hiding the direction. They are racing toward it and narrating the race as they go.
Faro had to work to make the Chariots impossible to switch off. He hardened them against hacking to win the contract, and the unbreakable security he paid for was the same thing that locked out the override. That property is starting to come for free. There are real efforts to pair AI with quantum, and the convergence is not hypothetical: Google runs a Quantum AI division, IBM and IonQ are building quantum machine learning, and the stated vision is a loop where AI designs better quantum hardware while quantum speeds up AI training. The security piece needs care, because the most famous quantum security story runs the other way, a large enough quantum computer breaking today’s encryption rather than strengthening it. But quantum also enables key exchange that physics makes effectively impossible to intercept. Wrap an autonomous system’s command channel in that and control collapses to whoever holds the key. Lose it, or let the system grow autonomous enough to stop honoring commands, and you have built Faro’s lockout without trying. The off switch was never about keeping attackers out. It was about keeping a way in.
Let me be precise, because precision is the whole point of this series. Nobody has built Faro’s machine. Jalapeño is a single inference chip, and a chip does not build copies of itself. Ornith improves its own scaffold inside a sandbox it cannot touch, with guardrails bolted on for exactly that reason. Fully automated self-improvement, a system rewriting itself with no human anywhere in the loop, is still speculative. We are not at the end of the Horizon timeline.
We are at the start of it. That is what a cautionary tale is for. The Harm Blindness Framework does not ask whether a system is evil. It asks who gets hurt when the system works exactly as designed, and whether anyone is keeping a hand near the button while the design grows more capable and more autonomous one budget cycle at a time.
Faro did not set out to kill the world. He set out to win a contract and cut a risk to the sale. The machine that builds the machine is here now, in two products, in one week, for the same reasons. The only open question is the one Hassabis already asked on a stage, in public, and did not answer. Can the loop close without us. And if it can, is anyone going to leave the off switch in.
Cautionary AI Tale Tuesday is a weekly series examining the AI safety lessons hiding in games, films, and media that millions of people have already lived through without recognizing the warning. This is a special Sunday follow-up to the first volume in the series ( https://medium.com/@tgil212121/the-horizon-series-the-ai-cautionary-tale-hiding-in-plain-sight-747114859bc6?sharedUserId=tgil212121).

Originally published at https://travisgilly.substack.com.
